coredata.dc:title: Surface charge induced tuning of electrical properties of CVD assisted graphene and functionalized graphene sheets
coredata.dc:creator: Deka, Manash Jyoti
Chowdhury, Devasish
coredata.dc:description: We demonstrate a new approach to tune the electrical properties of graphene and functionalized graphene. Graphene was synthesized using thermal chemical vapour deposition (TCVD) method on copper foil using precursor gas acetylene and co-catalyst H2 gas. TCVD assisted graphene was successfully transferred onto a silicon wafer. Transferred graphene sheet was then functionalized to prepare graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO). Different surface charge carbon nanoparticles, e.g. carbon nanoparticle with net positive charge and carbon nanoparticle with net negative charge were then immobilized on transferred graphene and functionalized graphene sheets. The functionalized graphene and charge mobilized functionalized graphene were characterized by Uv–vis spectroscopy, Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, and Raman spectroscopy. After immobilization of carbon nanomaterials, the ac electrical conductivity was found to increase due to enhancement of the surface charge, electron density, and mobility. It was observed that negative surface charge immobilized graphene and functionalized graphene show higher conductivity. Thus, the electrical property of graphene and functionalized graphene can be tuned by surface modification with different surface charge carbon nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Chemical
Vapour
Deposition (CVD)
Surface charge

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene using chemical vapor deposition method
Oxidation of graphene to using H2SO4/HNO3
Reduction of prepared GO to rGO
Preparation of carbon dots from tea (CDTea)
Preparation of carbon dots from chitosan (CDCh)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of transfer process of CVD grown graphene and synthesis of different surface charge immobilized carbon nanomaterials on graphene and functionalized graphene systems.
Schematic representation of the probable mechanism for the difference in conductivity of tea and chitosan-carbon dots immobilized graphene system.
Stacked Uv–vis ible spectra of (A) Grp, rGO, and GO, and (B) CDTea and CDCh.
Stacked FTIR spectra of (A) Grp, rGO and GO, (B) CDTeaand CDCh.

Representative SEM images of (A) bare copper surface annealed at 850 °C, (B) and (C) as-grown graphene on copper substrate, (D) transferred graphene on silicon substrate, (E) tea carbon dots (CDTea), and (F) chitosan-carbon dots (CDCh) immobilized on transfer graphene.
Stacked Raman spectra of (A) Grp (transfer) and (B) comparision of D and G peaks of Grp-CDCh and Grp-CDTea on a silicon wafer substrate.
(A) Stacked logZ vs logf plot of Grp, GO, and rGO, (B) the corresponding Nyquist diagram of graphene and functionalized graphene.
logZ vs logf plot of (A) Grp, Grp-CDCh, and Grp-CDTea, (B) GO, GO-CDCh and GO-CDTea, (C) rGO, rGO-CDCh and rGO-CDTea.
Calculation of I D/I G ratio of Grp-CDCh and Grp-CDTea.

head.ce:title: Surface charge induced tuning of electrical properties of CVD assisted graphene and functionalized graphene sheets

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Material Nanochemistry Laboratory, Physical Sciences Division, Institute of Advanced Study in Science and Technology, Paschim Boragaon, Garchuk, Guwahati, 781035, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Study in Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We demonstrate a new approach to tune the electrical properties of graphene and functionalized graphene. Graphene was synthesized using thermal chemical vapour deposition (TCVD) method on copper foil using precursor gas acetylene and co-catalyst H2 gas. TCVD assisted graphene was successfully transferred onto a silicon wafer. Transferred graphene sheet was then functionalized to prepare graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO). Different surface charge carbon nanoparticles, e.g. carbon nanoparticle with net positive charge and carbon nanoparticle with net negative charge were then immobilized on transferred graphene and functionalized graphene sheets. The functionalized graphene and charge mobilized functionalized graphene were characterized by Uv–vis spectroscopy, Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, and Raman spectroscopy. After immobilization of carbon nanomaterials, the ac electrical conductivity was found to increase due to enhancement of the surface charge, electron density, and mobility. It was observed that negative surface charge immobilized graphene and functionalized graphene show higher conductivity. Thus, the electrical property of graphene and functionalized graphene can be tuned by surface modification with different surface charge carbon nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, the newest member of the hybrid nanocarbon family arranged in a planar and 2-D [1,2] continuous network of hexagonal sp2 bonded carbon in the form of a honeycomb [3] draws the enormous interest of worldwide scientific researchers recently. It possesses some outstanding and exceptional properties like high Young's modulus [4], high thermal conductivity [5], optical transmittances [6], excellent carrier mobility [7], large specific surface area [8], quantum hall effect [9,10], long-range ballistic transport [11,12], adsorption of gas molecules [13], thermal stability [14] etc. These outstanding and superior properties and unique band gap [15,16] make graphene a promising 2D hybrid nanoscale building block for various systems. Graphene has shown many aspects of their wide-ranging fascinating applications in energy-storage devices [17,18], such as capacitors [19], solar cell [20], batteries [21], catalysis [22], new generation optoelectronic components [23], polymer nanocomposites [24], sensors [25,26], transparent conducting electrodes, surface coating [27,28] and mechanical resonators [29], drug delivery [30] and so forth.

Amongst several methods used to synthesize graphene after its discovery, the "bottom-up" synthesis strategy using CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition) from heterogeneous catalysis on transition metal substrates (e.g. copper, nickel, etc.) has possessed many advantages [31–34]. The main benefits of CVD grown graphene include the large surface area, high purity, fine-grained, very high quality of the continuous synthesis graphene and the number of exfoliated layers is also controllable [35].

The band gap tuning [36] is necessary to change the electrical properties of graphene by chemical modification strategy such as covalent and noncovalent functionalization approaches [37,38]. One of the approaches utilized to tune the electronic properties is through surface engineering graphene with other nanomaterials through functionalization [39], immobilization, encapsulation and fabricating nanocomposites. There are several reports on the synthesis of graphene/nanomaterials composites using different approaches for different electrochemical and impedimetric sensing applications. Geng and co-workers [40] have successfully utilized aqueous-processable noncovalent chemically converted graphene–quantum dot composites for flexible and transparent optoelectronic films. Yang et al. has demonstrated graphene oxide/carbon dot composite as a photoelectrode, which greatly suppresses the charge recombination and evidently enhances the photocurrent response activity [41]. Wang and co-workers have successfully assembled crystalline SnO2 immobilized graphene composite as a promising anode material to improve the performance of lithium-ion batteries [42]. A new electrochemical dopamine sensor based on the reduced graphene oxide (rGO)/carbon dot composite film with high sensitivity, specificity, and good stability was developed by Hu et al [43]. Wang and coworkers have investigated a novel strategy for the enhancement of electrochemiluminescence by combining CdSe quantum dots with graphene oxide/chitosan composites and was used to detect cytochrome C from glucose and bovine serum albumin [44]. Li et al. have successfully fabricated a graphene–CdSe quantum dots composite systems for sensitive electrogenerated chemiluminescence detection of human IgG (HIgG) [45]. But all these cases listed above chemically synthesize graphene and functionalized graphene were used. There are only a few reports on post-functionalization of CVD grown graphene for nanoelectronics and biosensors [46] applications. Wei et al. [47] successfully synthesized N-doped CVD grown graphene which exhibits an n-type behavior and shows substitutional doping can effectively modulate the electrical properties of graphene. Huang et al. [48] have demonstrated a nanoelectronic biosensor based on large-sized CVD grown graphene film functionalized with specific redox mediators for detection of glucose and glutamate. Eissa and co-workers [49] have modified functionalized CVD monolayer graphene for label-free impedimetric biosensing.

In this work, we employed ac impedance spectroscopy in details to measure the electrical properties immobilizing with different surface charge carbon dots on graphene and functionalized graphene systems. As we are functionalizing and immobilizing graphene with charged species, ac conductivity will account both the electrical component (electron movement) as well as ionic component (ions movement) and provide the total conductivity of this system [38].

In our previous reports [38,50], we have successfully exfoliated and covalently incorporated different functional groups on chemically modified graphene nanosheets and investigated the ac electrical conductivity with different functionality and the lateral dimension. And following the same protocol we successfully tuned the electrical properties of graphene interacted with various π-stacking organic molecules. We realized that to use graphene and functionalized graphene as electronic material in optoelectronic devices it is very important that we can tune the electrical property of these materials. To achieve that bandgap tuning of graphene and functionalized graphene are necessary. In the present work, we have demonstrated that the tuning the electrical properties of graphene and functionalized graphene is also possible simply by surface modification with immobilization different surface charged carbon nanomaterials. This is the first report of direct utilization of CVD growth graphene and functionalized graphene to tune the ac electrical properties immobilizing with different surface charge carbon dots. As our group has already reported, synthesis of different surface charge carbon dots from tea [51,52] and chitosan hydrogel [53] precursors. We successfully immobilized them on CVD assisted graphene and functionalized graphene by drop casting method. After immobilization of different surface charge carbon nanomaterials, the ac electrical conductivity of these systems increases the electron density, surface charge, and mobility. Thus, the electrical property of graphene and functionalized graphene can be tuned by surface modification with different surface charge carbon nanomaterials.

Cu foil (25-μm-thick, square area of 6 cm2, 99.8%) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Hydrazine hydrate (H6N2O), sulphuric acid (H2SO4), nitric acid (HNO3) hydrochloric acid (HCl) and ferric chloride (FeCl3) were purchased from Merck, India. All the chemicals were used as obtained without further purification. The water used throughout the experiments was from a Milli-Q water purification system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene using chemical vapor deposition method
ce:section.ce:para: Continuous graphene films were grown on the copper substrate by a customized CVD system (Technos Instruments, India) with a quartz tube furnace [54]. C2H2 served as the carbon precursor and Cu foil (Alfa Aesar, 25-μm-thick, square area of 6 cm2, 99.8%) served as the catalyst. The Cu foils were cleaned by an ultrasonic cleaner for 15 min in alcohol and acetone to remove the oxide layers from copper. The synthesis process by CVD can be described as follows: the Cu foil was loaded in a quartz tube and pumped down to 5 × 10−2 Torr. Then samples were annealed to 850 °C at a rate of 15 °C/min under the constant flowing of 100 standard-state cubic centimeters per minute (sccm) Ar. During the growth period 60 sccm H2 and 6 sccm C2H2 were introduced (H2:C2H2 = 10:1). The grown time was set to 2 min. Finally, the sample was cooled down to room temperature at a rate of 20 °C/min under Ar atmosphere as the growth condition.

Cu foils with grown graphene films were separated from copper foil by dissolving these foils in 1 M FeCl3 etchant solution. After dissolution, the graphene sheets remained floating on the solution, is carefully washed by placing it in a Petri dish of DI water and then transferred onto silicon wafer substrates (1 cm × 1 cm) for further characterization and electrical measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidation of graphene to using H2SO4/HNO3
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene was oxidized in a single step using a simple chemical reaction already employed for CNT oxidation [55]. Here, an acid mixture i.e., a 3:1 mixture of H2SO4/HNO3, has been used as an oxidizing agent. Typically graphene transfer on silicon was dipped in a 3:1 acid mixture of concentrated H2SO4 (98 wt%)/HNO3(16 M) for 12 h. Then it was washed with deionized water for several cycles and dried in oven at 90 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduction of prepared GO to rGO
ce:section.ce:para: Chemical reduction of GO was carried out by dipping the as prepared GO on the silicon wafer in 20 ml (30%) hydrazine hydrate solution for 8 h. In the reduction step, GO is transformed into reduced GO (rGO) by the elimination of the oxygenated functional groups. Then it was washed with deionized water for several cycles and dried in an oven at 90 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots from tea (CDTea)
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots from tea (CDTea) were prepared according to a standard protocol developed and reported by our laboratory [51]. Briefly, commercially available Assam tea was first heated at 200 °C for about 2 h, followed by grinding to powdered form and again heating at 200 °C for about 8 h. The so formed black carbonized powder of tea was cooled to room temperature and stored in a glass vial. 0.1 M acetic acid was used as the dispersion medium for the preparation of CDs from tea. A quantity of 300 mg of the carbonized tea was dispersed in10 ml of 0.1 M acetic acid and kept for 40 h. The dispersed medium was then centrifuged (at 10,000 rpm for 0.5 h), and the supernatant liquid containing tea CDs were collected and preserved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots from chitosan (CDCh)
ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan hydrogel was also synthesized as reported earlier by our group [51]. Briefly, 1% glacial acetic acid solution and glycerol were mixed in the ratio of 1 part acetic acid solution to 3 parts glycerol to form a solvent. 0.1 g chitosan was dissolved in 10 ml of the solvent above by stirring with a magnetic stirrer at room temperature for 1–2 h to form a clear pale yellow solution. The solution was neutralized by adding 300 μl 5 N NaOH. Immediately a clear, slightly tacky gel was formed. The gel, which apparently results from the interaction of chitosan, glycerol, and water, has a three-dimensional structure, and no free water or glycerol exists.

To make the chitosan CDs, a small part of the Chitosan hydrogel was taken and dissolved in 0.1 M acetic acid and heated in a microwave for 20 min. The formation of CDs was confirmed by bluish glow when placed under UV-light. (Fig. S1 in supplementary material)

The characterization of graphene, functionalized graphene, and carbon dots was carried out by UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2600) in solid and liquid mode, dynamic light scattering (Malvern Zetasizer Nano series, Nano ZS90), Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 6700), scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Carl Zeiss ∑igma-VP), Raman analysis were performed using laser micro-raman system, Horiba Jobin Vyon, Model LabRam HR, using 514 nm laser at room temperature. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Jasco spectrofluorometer FP-8300. All ac impedance measurements were recorded in CH Instruments, Electrochemical workstation using SEMI PROBE MA-8000 system at room temperature in a frequency range of 0.1 Hz to 5 MHz at the constant voltage of 1 V in ambient conditions. The experiment was carried out in a relative humidity of 55%.

In this work, we tuned the electrical properties of graphene and functionalized graphene by surface modification with immobilizing different surface charge carbon nanoparticles (carbon dots). The schematic illustration of the transfer process of CVD growth graphene to a silicon wafer and synthesis of different surface charge immobilized carbon nanomaterials on graphene and functionalized graphene to tune the electrical properties are shown in Scheme 1 .

Fig. 1 (A) shows the stacked UV–vis spectra of Grp, GO and rGO on silicon wafer initially recorded in percentage reflectance mode from 600 to 200 nm, which was then converted into absorbance mode using Kubelka-Munk (KM) transformation. From Fig. 1(A), it can be seen that for transferred graphene there is a characteristics peak near 202 nm in the UV–vis region due to π-π * transition of CC bond but after oxidation with 1:3 (HNO3:H2SO4), two characteristics peaks appeared. The peak near 298 nm is due to n- π * transition of OH and COOH groups and a peak near 230 nm can be assigned to π - π * transition of the CC groups, which confirms the successful incorporation of oxygenated functional groups on transferred graphene nanosheets. But after reduction of GO to rGO using 30% hydrazine hydrate the peaks near 298 nm disappeared and the peak at 230 was shifted to 260 and intensity of this peak was increased, which suggest the regeneration of CC bond and removal of oxygen-containing functional groups gradually from the graphene nanosheets [56]. Thus, from UV–vis spectra, it is confirmed that there is a successful functionalization of graphene to form GO and rGO.

Fig. 1(B) depicts the UV–vis spectra of chitosan-carbon dots (CDCh) and tea carbon dots (CDTea). It is evident from the spectra that for chitosan-carbon dots (CDCh), the characteristic peak is near 204 nm, which is due to n-σ* transitions of OH and NH2 groups on the carbon dots surface. Again, tea carbon dots (CDTea) exhibit two characteristics peaks. The peak near 275 nm is due to n- π* OH groups on the surface of carbon dots and the other peak near 207 nm that can be assigned to π- π * transitions of the aromatic systems in the polyphenols.

FTIR spectroscopy demonstrates the successful functionalization of transfer graphene sheet and subsequent functionalization with different functional moiety. Fig. 2 (A) shows the stacked FTIR spectra of Grp, rGO, GO. The FTIR spectra show that no major peak was observed for transfer graphene but upon oxidation four prominent peaks appeared at 3430, 1790, 1645 and 1342 cm−1. The peak near 3430 cm−1 is due to −OH stretching vibrations, peak at 1790 cm−1 indicates CO stretching vibrations, a peak near 1645 cm−1 shows CC stretching vibrations from unoxidized sp2 domains of CC bonds and the peak at 1342 cm−1 C–O stretching vibrations [57,58]. It is also interesting to note that on the reduction of GO to form rGO all the significant peaks were reduced in intensity due to the removal of all the oxygenated functional groups from the GO surface. Thus, FTIR study confirms the successful functionalization on graphene nanosheets to GO and rGO.

We have also characterized the chemical structure of prepared CDTea and CDCh. Fig. 2(B) depicts the FTIR spectra of CDTea and CDCh. For CDTea, the prominent peak at 3406 cm−1 is due to OH stretching vibration of polyphenols in tea carbon dots. The characteristics peaks at 1660 and1565 cm−1 arose from in-ring stretching vibrations of CC of the tea polyphenols. The other peaks at 1410, 1238 and 1120 cm−1 can be assigned to CH bending, CO stretching vibrations of phenol and ether groups respectively and another characteristics peak at 619 is due to CH stretching vibration of the aromatic counterparts of tea carbon dots system [51].

From Fig. 2(B), it is seen that the main prominent peaks of CDCh observed at 3406, 2925, 2852, 1635, 1315, 1054 cm−1. The peak at 3406 cm-1 is due to NH and OH stretching vibrations, peaks at 2925 and 2852 cm-1 arose from C–H stretching vibration, 1635 peak is due to NH bending vibration, peaks between 1480-1200 is for CC, CN groups, and the peak at 1054 cm−1 can be C–H bending vibrations of the pyranose ring present in chitosan-carbon dots [59].

Fluorescence spectroscopy also confirms the formation of CDTea and CDCh. It is interesting to note that CDTea and CDCh show excitation dependent PL behavior like other CDs prepared from different precursors molecules [60,61]. The PL spectra obtained after excitation of CDTea at 300 nm to 420 nm shows that with an increase in the excitation wavelength the PL intensity first increased and shifted to the higher wavelength (lower energy) and then intensity gradually decreased. But for CDCh excitation at 300 nm shows the highest intensity and then it is shifted and intensity gradually decreased. The stacked emission spectra of CDTea and CD Ch are given in Fig. S1 (supplementary material). The PL spectra depict the synthesis of CDTea and CDCh are successful.

The surface charges of tea and chitosan-carbon dots came from zeta potential value, which is tabulated in Table S1 (supplementary material). The zeta potential values for tea and chitosan-carbon dots are found to be −115 mV and + 30.2 mV, respectively. From DLS measurements, it is found that the particle size of tea and chitosan-carbon dots are below 10 nm and depicted in Fig. S2 (supplementary material), which is in good agreement with SEM images of CDTea and CDCh.

The CVD growth graphene and carbon dots immobilized transfer graphene were further characterized by SEM. Fig. 3 (A) represents the bare copper substrate annealed at 850 °C and depicts its uniform crystallinity. Fig. 3(B and C) shows the representative SEM images of graphene grown on the copper substrate at 850 °C using precursor gas C2H2. The images reveal that the graphene domains are continuous, uniform and clean but Cu grain boundaries and some wrinkles are generated, and a typical lateral size of each graphene domain is found to be 15–20 μm. In the cooling step in CVD process, these wrinkles are generated due to thermal expansion of graphene [62]. The transferred graphene on a silicon substrate is shown in Fig. 3(D) and from the SEM image it is confirmed that the graphene domains are continuous and lateral dimensions are 15–20 μm. Fig. 3(E and F) depicts the representative SEM images of tea and chitosan-carbon dots(CDTea and CDCh) immobilized graphene. From the SEM micrograph, it is observed that carbon dots uniformly distributed on transfer graphene surfaces.

Fig. 4 (A) depicts the Raman spectra of transfer graphene on a silicon substrate. The existence of the band near 1582 cm−l (G-band) is due to E 2g vibrational mode of sp2 bonded carbon of graphitic structure, and the characteristic band near 1360 cm-1 (D-band) arose from a disordered structure in the carbon network. The 2D band near 2730 cm-1 is the secondary D peak and originates from second order double resonant process of graphene. Raman spectra of carbon dots-immobilized graphene also show structural features of the carbon nanomaterials themselves as those are also carbonaceous. Therefore, we cannot make any comparison of non-decorated and decorated carbon dots. From Fig. 4(B) it is seen that the broad peaks and higher I D/I G indicate that the Raman signal mainly arises from the immobilized carbon dots, which implies that the nanoparticles are decorated and immobilized uniformly on the graphene surface. The amount of disorder (I D/I G) of carbon dots immobilize graphene system is tabulated in Table 1 .

As discussed earlier we prepared three solid systems of different carbon nanomaterials (CDTea and CDCh) immobilized graphene and functionalized graphene supported on the silicon wafer (1 cm × 1 cm) substrate and measured its impedance. The impedance measurements were carried out in a CH Instrument, Electrochemical workstation using SEMI PROBE MA-8000 system at room temperature in a frequency range ftom 0.1 Hz to 5 MHz at the constant voltage of 1 V at ambient conditions. The experiment was carried out in a relative humidity of 55%. In SEMI PROBE MA-8000 system two pointed pin to act as an electrode, which is made contact with graphene and functionalized graphene on silicon wafer substrates (1 cm × 1 cm) perpendicularly. The wafer with graphene and functionalized graphene was connected to the pointed pin (act as electrodes) of SEMI PROBE using conductive silver paste on both ends of the silicon wafer. In general, impedance (Z) is defined as the frequency domain ratio of the voltage to the current. In other words, it is the voltage-current ratio for a single complex exponential at a particular frequency. Impedance has the same unit as resistance and it is a complex number. For a sinusoidal current or voltage input, the polar form of the complex impedance relates to the amplitude and phase of the voltage and current. Impedance is inversely related to conductivity. In other words, increase in impedance means decrease in conductivity and vice versa.

Fig. 5 (A) shows the dependence of impedance with frequency of different functionalized graphene. The plot was recorded for Grp, GO, and rGO. The plot demonstrates that ac conductivity rGO was determined to be the highest. For GO upon functionalization, due to the introduction of bulky groups onto the graphene sheets, its conductivity changes to a lower value due to structural disorder and distraction on the delocalized structure.

Fig. 5(B) shows the Nyquist diagram of graphene and functionalized graphene. In Nyquist plots, Zcosθ is plotted against Zsinθ, where Z is the impedance and θ is the phase angle. Zcosθ is the real part and Zsinθ is the imaginary part and denoted by Z′ and Z″, respectively. The intersecting point of the two semicircles of complex impedance plot coincides with the bulk resistance (R b) of the sample. The semicircles coinciding with the charge transfer resistance limiting process is associated with the graphene and reduced graphene oxide mainly which observes the highest ac conductivity. On introduction different functional moieties on graphene, the semicircles become extensive, suggesting lower conductivity of functionalized graphene, which is in agreements with our previous report [38].

Fig. 6 (A) shows the dependence of impedance with frequency of different functionalized graphene sheet. The plot was recorded for graphene and different surface charge immobilized carbon dots on graphene. This study is also performed for functionalized graphene and carbon nanomaterials immobilized functionalized graphene.

The plot demonstrates that ac conductivity of Grp-CDTea was determined to be the highest compared to bare graphene and Grp-CDCh. It is well known that graphene has planar and hexagonal structures having an alternate CC and CC bonds and so delocalization of sp2 hybridized carbon electrons is possible. On immobilization with negatively charged tea carbon dot on graphene surface its total surface area as well as total electron density and mobility increases. But in the case of immobilized chitosan-carbon dot system, which has positive surface charge, there is a neutralization of electric charge with delocalized electron of graphene. However, all the surface charge of graphene may not be neutralized. In this case, also a total surface area of the system increases. This is the reason for the observed enhancement of ac electrical conductivity of tea carbon dot immobilized on the graphene surface. But both the systems show better electrical conductivity than Grp. The trend of observed conductivity values is Grp-CDTea > Grp-CDCh > Grp. From Fig. 6(B and C) it is also evident that the observed trend is also true for carbon dots immobilized GO and rGO systems. For functionalized graphene GO-CDTea > GO-CDCh > GO and rGO-CDTea > rGO-CDCh > rGO

We propose a simple mechanism (Scheme 2 ) to explain the enhancement of ac electrical conductivity (lower impedance) of immobilized surface charge graphene Grp-CDTea and Grp-CDCh. Zeta potential (surface charge) of the synthesized fluorescent carbon dot plays an important role in enhancing the electrical conductivity. It is known that graphene has planar and hexagonal structures have an alternate CC and CC bonds and so delocalization of sp2 hybridized carbon electrons is possible. On immobilization with negatively charged tea carbon dot on graphene surface, its total surface area, electron density, surface charge as well as electron mobility increases. But in the case of chitosan-carbon dot system, which has positive surface charge, there is a neutralization of electric charge with delocalized electron of graphene. This is the reason for the observed enhancement of ac electrical conductivity of tea carbon dot immobilized graphene. This fact is also valid for carbon dots immobilized functionalized graphene systems.

In this work, electrical properties of CVD assisted graphene and functionalized graphene immobilized with a different surface charged carbon dots were studied. Continuous graphene films were grown on the copper substrate by a customized CVD system with a quartz tube furnace. The C2H2 acted as carbon source and Cu foil as catalyst for the graphene growth. The carbon dots used in this study for immobilization on graphene were mostly chitosan and tea origin. Interestingly chitosan carbon dots show positive zeta potential (surface charge) value and tea carbon dots possess negative zeta potential (surface charge). These immobilized carbon dots on graphene show the higher ac conductivity than graphene. The improvement of conductivity shown can be attributed to higher surface charge, and the density of π - electron and higher surface area of the immobilized system. This reason is also valid for carbon dots immobilized functionalized graphene (GO and rGO) systems. Negative surface charged carbon dots (CDTea) induced graphene to possess higher electrical conductivity. Thus it is possible to tune the electrical properties of graphene and functionalized graphene through surface modification by immobilizing with different surface charge carbon dots. These types of approaches need further investigation to use in optoelectronic devices in near future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), New Delhi, for project grant SB/S1/PC- 69/2012 and the Board of Research in Nuclear Sciences (BRNS), Mumbai, Grant No. 34/14/20/2014- BRNS. MJD wants to thank SERB, New Delhi, for fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jmst.2018.09.017.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
